title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who took part in citizen science projects. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Helen, The Bird Counter
Helen joined a citizen science bird count on the estuary after her doctor suggested more outdoor activity and a friend offered her spare binoculars. She expected gentle walks, not the discipline of standing still for twenty minutes in freezing wind. [47]Her first mistake was counting the same flock twice after it circled back over the mudflats.[/47] An experienced volunteer showed her how to note direction, time and behaviour, not just numbers, because movement often mattered more than a final total. Helen found the routine calming, especially because she had spent years making quick decisions in a hospital reception. [48]That background helped her stay polite when impatient walkers asked why the footpath had been temporarily closed.[/48] Over time, she became fascinated by small changes, such as birds feeding closer to the town after storms. She says the project taught her that patience can produce information no single photograph would capture, particularly during an ordinary winter morning.
texts.B:
### Idris, The River Sampler
Idris collected water samples from three points along the river every Friday morning before work, carrying them in a small insulated lunch bag. [49]He chose the earliest shift because factory discharges were easier to detect before boats stirred up the water.[/49] The process looked simple to passers-by, but the bottles had to be rinsed, labelled and cooled in exactly the right order. [50]He once had to discard a full set of samples because one label had become damp and unreadable.[/50] Idris, who teaches chemistry, enjoyed turning abstract lessons about pollution into real data from his own town, which made his students pay attention. What surprised him most was the smell of the river changing after heavy rain, when drains overflowed near the old bridge. He says the figures were sometimes depressing, but they gave campaigners evidence that could not be dismissed as a vague complaint from worried residents at public meetings.
texts.C:
### Marta, The Night-Sky Mapper
Marta volunteered for a project measuring local light pollution after moving to a flat where she could barely see the stars from her kitchen window. [51]She had to take readings at midnight, when street lighting was steady and most shop signs had been switched off.[/51] The hour was inconvenient on work nights, but she liked the quiet streets and the occasional fox crossing empty roads near the library car park. As a photographer, she understood exposure settings, yet [52]she was surprised that the official device rejected any reading if her hand shook slightly.[/52] Marta eventually began resting it on a folded scarf to keep it level. Her favourite result was persuading one sports club to angle its floodlights downward, which reduced glare across three nearby streets. She says the project gave residents a way to talk about darkness as something valuable locally, not simply a lack of safety or activity.
texts.D:
### Owen, The Butterfly Recorder
Owen started recording butterflies on a disused railway line because his daughter needed a nature project for school during a wet half-term holiday. [53]He continued after she lost interest, mainly because identifying tiny differences forced him to slow down.[/53] The most difficult species were not rare ones, but common brown butterflies that changed appearance as their wings aged. [54]To avoid unreliable guesses, he photographed the underside of each wing before submitting a record.[/54] Owen had worked as a delivery driver, so he knew shortcuts between survey sites and could cover three areas in one afternoon without rushing the observations. He was embarrassed when a submitted sighting was rejected, but the reviewer sent a kind explanation and links to comparison images. [55]He now keeps a list of rejected records to remind himself which features he misread.[/55] [56]His proudest moment was seeing the railway line added to a council map of wildlife corridors, protecting it from a proposed shortcut road.[/56]

questions.question: mentions initially making an error because the same animals returned to view?
questions.explanation: Helen counted the same flock twice after it circled back.

questions.question: used experience from a public-facing job to handle questions calmly?
questions.explanation: Helen's hospital reception background helped her stay polite with impatient walkers.

questions.question: selected a particular time because it made evidence easier to detect?
questions.explanation: Idris chose the earliest shift because discharges were easier to detect then.

questions.question: had to throw away data because identifying information was damaged?
questions.explanation: Idris discarded samples after a label became damp and unreadable.

questions.question: needed to collect information late at night for technical consistency?
questions.explanation: Marta had to take readings at midnight when lighting conditions were steady.

questions.question: was surprised by how sensitive the measuring equipment was to movement?
questions.explanation: Marta found the device rejected readings if her hand shook slightly.

questions.question: kept volunteering after the family member who prompted the activity stopped?
questions.explanation: Owen continued after his daughter lost interest.

questions.question: used photographs to prevent uncertain identifications from being submitted?
questions.explanation: Owen photographed butterfly wings before submitting a record.

questions.question: keeps notes about previous mistakes to avoid repeating them?
questions.explanation: Owen keeps a list of rejected records to remember misread features.

questions.question: was pleased when the survey site gained official recognition on a local plan?
questions.explanation: Owen was proud when the railway line was added to a council map.
